axiom ST_netflix : Netflix ST
axiom ST_popular : Popular ST

axiom R1 : ∀ s : Show, Netflix s ∧ Popular s → Binge s

  have hN : Netflix ST := ST_netflix
  have hP : Popular ST := ST_popular
  have hB : Binge ST := R1 ST ⟨hN, hP⟩
  exact R3 ST ⟨hN, hB⟩

axiom ComplainOften : obj → Prop
axiom SibsBornTogether : obj → Prop

-- Rules from the context
axiom R1 : ∀ x : obj, Multiple x → SpendWithSiblings x
axiom R2 : ∀ x : obj, SpendWithSiblings x → LiveAtHome x
axiom R3 : ∀ x : obj, LiveAtHome x → ¬ LiveWithStrangers x
axiom R4 : ∀ x : obj, ComplainOften x → SibsBornTogether x
axiom R5 : ∀ x : obj, SibsBornTogether x → Multiple x

-- Disjunction about Luke
axiom luke_disj : (Multiple Luke ∧ LiveWithStrangers Luke) ∨ (¬ Multiple Luke ∧ ¬ LiveWithStrangers Luke)

theorem luke_neither : ¬ Multiple Luke ∧ ¬ ComplainOften Luke := by
  -- Show the first disjunct leads to a contradiction
  have not_first : ¬ (Multiple Luke ∧ LiveWithStrangers Luke) := by
    intro h
    have hM : Multiple Luke := And.left h
    have hLS : LiveWithStrangers Luke := And.right h
    have hSp : SpendWithSiblings Luke := R1 Luke hM
    have hLH : LiveAtHome Luke := R2 Luke hSp
    have hNotLS : ¬ LiveWithStrangers Luke := R3 Luke hLH
    exact hNotLS hLS
  -- From the disjunction, obtain ¬Multiple Luke ∧ ¬LiveWithStrangers Luke
  have nm_and_nls : ¬ Multiple Luke ∧ ¬ LiveWithStrangers Luke := by
    cases luke_disj with
    | inl h =>
        have f : False := not_first h
        exact False.elim f
    | inr h => exact h
  have nM : ¬ Multiple Luke := And.left nm_and_nls
  -- Use ComplainOften → SibsBornTogether → Multiple to contradict ¬Multiple
  have nC : ¬ ComplainOften Luke := by
    intro hC
    have hMB : Multiple Luke := R5 Luke (R4 Luke hC)
    exact nM hMB
  exact And.intro nM nC
</lean>

axiom obj : Type
axiom Luke : obj

axiom MultipleBirth : obj → Prop
axiom PlaySibs : obj → Prop
axiom HaveSibsBornTogether : obj → Prop
axiom ComplainOften : obj → Prop
axiom LiveAtHome : obj → Prop
axiom LiveWithStrangers : obj → Prop

-- People who are born in a multiple birth with siblings spend a lot of time with their siblings.
axiom A1 : ∀ x : obj, MultipleBirth x → PlaySibs x
-- If people have siblings who were born together, then they were born in a multiple birth.
axiom A2 : ∀ x : obj, HaveSibsBornTogether x → MultipleBirth x
-- If people complain often about annoying siblings, then they have siblings who were born together.
axiom A3 : ∀ x : obj, ComplainOften x → HaveSibsBornTogether x
-- If people live at home, then they do not live with strangers.
axiom A4 : ∀ x : obj, LiveAtHome x → ¬ LiveWithStrangers x
-- If people spend a lot of time with their siblings, then they often live at home.
axiom A5 : ∀ x : obj, PlaySibs x → LiveAtHome x

-- Luke either is born in a multiple birth and lives with strangers, or is not born in a multiple birth and does not live with strangers.
axiom A6 : (MultipleBirth Luke ∧ LiveWithStrangers Luke) ∨ (¬ MultipleBirth Luke ∧ ¬ LiveWithStrangers Luke)

-- Derivable helper: being born in a multiple birth implies not living with strangers.
lemma M_implies_notS : ∀ x : obj, MultipleBirth x → ¬ LiveWithStrangers x := by
  intro x hM
  have hPlay : PlaySibs x := A1 x hM
  have hHome : LiveAtHome x := A5 x hPlay
  exact A4 x hHome

-- Hence the left disjunct in A6 is impossible.
lemma left_impossible : ¬ (MultipleBirth Luke ∧ LiveWithStrangers Luke) := by
  intro h
  have hM : MultipleBirth Luke := And.left h
  have hS : LiveWithStrangers Luke := And.right h
  have hNotS : ¬ LiveWithStrangers Luke := M_implies_notS Luke hM
  exact hNotS hS

-- Therefore the right disjunct must hold: Luke is not born in a multiple birth and does not live with strangers.
theorem luke_not_multiple_and_not_strangers : (¬ MultipleBirth Luke ∧ ¬ LiveWithStrangers Luke) := by
  have disj := A6
  apply Or.elim disj
  · intro hleft
    have : False := by
      exact left_impossible hleft
    exact False.elim this
  · intro hright
    exact hright

-- Cannot prove PlaySibs Luke from given axioms
</lean>

axiom A2 : ∀ x : obj, ClumsyFoodieGoOut x → Spill x
-- If people are cleanly, then they are notably tidy.
axiom A3 : ∀ x : obj, Cleanly x → NotablyTidy x
-- If people value order and spotlessness, then they are cleanly.
axiom A4 : ∀ x : obj, ValueOrder x → Cleanly x
-- All people whose families prioritize order and spotlessness value order and spotlessness.
axiom A5 : ∀ x : obj, FamilyPrioritize x → ValueOrder x
-- Peter is either (spills and cleanly) or (not spills and not cleanly).

  have hnC : ¬ Cleanly Peter := (not_spill_and_not_cleanly).right
  intro hfp
  have hc : Cleanly Peter := A4 Peter (A5 Peter hfp)
  exact hnC hc

-- Hence the desired conclusion (CFGO Peter ∨ FamilyPrioritize Peter) is false.
theorem conclusion_false : ¬ (ClumsyFoodieGoOut Peter ∨ FamilyPrioritize Peter) := by
  intro h
  cases h with
  | inl hcfg => exact not_CFGO_Peter hcfg
  | inr hfp  => exact not_FP_Peter hfp

-- Cannot prove ClumsyFoodieGoOut Peter ∨ FamilyPrioritize Peter from the given premises.
</lean>

axiom Spill : obj → Prop
axiom Tidy : obj → Prop

axiom ValueOrder : obj → Prop
axiom FamilyPrioritizes : obj → Prop

-- Context axioms
axiom A1 : ∀ x : obj, Spill x → ¬ Tidy x
axiom A2 : ∀ x : obj, CFGO x → Spill x
axiom A3 : ∀ x : obj, Clean x → Tidy x
axiom A4 : ∀ x : obj, ValueOrder x → Clean x
axiom A5 : ∀ x : obj, FamilyPrioritizes x → ValueOrder x
axiom A6 : (Spill Peter ∧ Clean Peter) ∨ (¬ Spill Peter ∧ ¬ Clean Peter)

theorem statement :
  (CFGO Peter ∧ FamilyPrioritizes Peter) ∨ (¬ CFGO Peter ∧ ¬ FamilyPrioritizes Peter) := by
  -- Show (Spill Peter ∧ Clean Peter) is impossible
  have hcontr : Spill Peter ∧ Clean Peter → False := by
    intro hsc
    have s : Spill Peter := hsc.left
    have c : Clean Peter := hsc.right
    have nt : ¬ Tidy Peter := A1 Peter s
    have t : Tidy Peter := A3 Peter c
    exact nt t
  -- From the given disjunction, deduce ¬Spill Peter ∧ ¬Clean Peter
  have hNotSNotC : ¬ Spill Peter ∧ ¬ Clean Peter := by
    apply Or.elim A6
    · intro hsc
      exact False.elim (hcontr hsc)
    · intro h
      exact h
  have nS : ¬ Spill Peter := hNotSNotC.left
  have nC : ¬ Clean Peter := hNotSNotC.right

  -- From ¬Spill and CFGO → Spill, get ¬CFGO
  have nCfgo : ¬ CFGO Peter := by
    intro hcfgo
    have sp : Spill Peter := A2 Peter hcfgo
    exact nS sp

  -- FamilyPrioritizes → ValueOrder → Clean, so from ¬Clean get ¬FamilyPrioritizes
  have Fp_implies_Clean : ∀ x : obj, FamilyPrioritizes x → Clean x := by
    intro x hfp
    have vo : ValueOrder x := A5 x hfp
    exact A4 x vo
  have nFp : ¬ FamilyPrioritizes Peter := by
    intro hfp
    have cl : Clean Peter := Fp_implies_Clean Peter hfp
    exact nC cl

  exact Or.inr ⟨nCfgo, nFp⟩

-- All of Kelly Wearstler's design styles that Max adores are dreamy.

  have hBE : BrutalistBuilding x ∧ Evocative x := A6 x (And.intro hDM (And.intro hA hI))
  have hB : BrutalistBuilding x := And.left hBE
  have hNotI : ¬ InterestingGeometries x := A2 x (And.intro hB hA)
  exact hNotI hI

</lean>

<lean>
axiom obj : Type
axiom ZahaStyle : obj → Prop
axiom KellyStyle : obj → Prop
axiom Brutalist : obj → Prop
axiom Interesting : obj → Prop
axiom Evocative : obj → Prop
axiom Dreamy : obj → Prop
axiom ByMax : obj → Prop
axiom Adores : obj → Prop
axiom Style : obj → Prop

-- All of Zaha Hadid's design styles that Max adores have interesting geometries.
axiom A1 : ∀ x : obj, ZahaStyle x ∧ Adores x → Interesting x

-- No brutalist buildings that Max adores have interesting geometries.
axiom A2 : ∀ x : obj, Brutalist x ∧ Adores x → ¬ Interesting x

-- Every style that Max adores is either Zaha Hadid's design style or Kelly Wearstler's design style.
axiom A3 : ∀ x : obj, Style x ∧ Adores x → (ZahaStyle x ∨ KellyStyle x)

-- All of Kelly Wearstler's design styles that Max adores are evocative.
axiom A4 : ∀ x : obj, KellyStyle x ∧ Adores x → Evocative x

-- All of Kelly Wearstler's design styles that Max adores are dreamy.
axiom A5 : ∀ x : obj, KellyStyle x ∧ Adores x → Dreamy x

-- If a design by Max that he adores has interesting geometries, then it is a brutalist building and evocative.
axiom A6 : ∀ x : obj, ByMax x ∧ Adores x ∧ Interesting x → Brutalist x ∧ Evocative x

-- From A2 and A6, we can derive that there is no design by Max that he adores which is interesting.
theorem no_interesting_adored_design_by_Max : ∀ x : obj, ByMax x ∧ Adores x ∧ Interesting x → False := by
  intro x h
  have hx_by : ByMax x := And.left h
  have hx_ad_int : Adores x ∧ Interesting x := And.right h
  have hx_ad : Adores x := And.left hx_ad_int
  have hx_int : Interesting x := And.right hx_ad_int
  have hx_brut_ev : Brutalist x ∧ Evocative x := A6 x h
  have hx_brut : Brutalist x := And.left hx_brut_ev
  have not_int : ¬ Interesting x := A2 x (And.intro hx_brut hx_ad)
  exact not_int hx_int

-- However, from the given premises we cannot prove:
-- ∀ x, ByMax x → Evocative x ∨ Dreamy x
-- There is no link from "design by Max" to KellyStyle (which would yield Evocative/Dreamy),
-- and the only rule (A6) that yields Evocative requires Interesting, which leads to contradiction with A2,
-- providing no way to conclude Evocative or Dreamy for arbitrary designs by Max.
-- Therefore, the target statement is not derivable from the premises.
</lean>

ANSWER: Failure

    have hRafa : LostToRafa Coco := A5 Coco hAM

axiom Taller : obj → obj → Prop
axiom CanBlock : obj → obj → Prop
axiom JumpsWhenShooting : obj → Prop
axiom GreatShooter : obj → Prop

axiom Michael : obj
axiom Peter : obj
axiom Windy : obj

axiom InMichaelsClass : obj → Prop

-- If a man is taller than another man, the taller one can block the other's shooting.
axiom T1 : ∀ x y : obj, Man x → Man y → Taller x y → CanBlock x y

-- Michael is a man who is taller than everyone else in his class.
axiom T2a : Man Michael
axiom T2b : ∀ y : obj, InMichaelsClass y → Taller Michael y

-- If person x is taller than person y, and person y is taller than person z, then x is taller than z
axiom T3 : ∀ x y z : obj, Taller x y → Taller y z → Taller x z

-- Peter is a man who is taller than Michael.
axiom T4a : Man Peter
axiom T4b : Taller Peter Michael

-- Michael can block any shooting from a person as long as the person does not jump when shooting.
axiom T5 : ∀ p : obj, ¬ JumpsWhenShooting p → CanBlock Michael p

-- Michael cannot block Windy's shooting.
axiom T6 : ¬ CanBlock Michael Windy

-- Every shooter who can jump when shooting is a great shooter.
axiom T7 : ∀ p : obj, JumpsWhenShooting p → GreatShooter p

theorem Windy_is_great_shooter : GreatShooter Windy :=
by
  classical
  have hem : JumpsWhenShooting Windy ∨ ¬ JumpsWhenShooting Windy := Classical.em (JumpsWhenShooting Windy)
  exact
    match hem with
    | Or.inl hjump => T7 Windy hjump
    | Or.inr hnojump =>
        have hb : CanBlock Michael Windy := T5 Windy hnojump
        have : False := T6 hb
        False.elim this

axiom obj : Type
axiom John : obj

axiom Engaged : obj → Prop
axiom MakePlans : obj → Prop
axiom InviteOthers : obj → Prop
axiom WellAttended : obj → Prop
axiom LargerFamily : obj → Prop
axiom InviteFriends : obj → Prop

-- 1. All people who make wedding plans are engaged.
axiom A1 : ∀ x : obj, MakePlans x → Engaged x
-- 2. All people who invite others to come to their ceremony make wedding plans.
axiom A2 : ∀ x : obj, InviteOthers x → MakePlans x
-- 3. Anyone who has a well-attended wedding invites others to come to their ceremony.
axiom A3 : ∀ x : obj, WellAttended x → InviteOthers x
-- 4. Some people that have well-attended weddings have larger families.
axiom A4 : ∃ x : obj, WellAttended x ∧ LargerFamily x
-- 5. If John is engaged, then John does not invite friends to their ceremony and does not make wedding plans.
axiom A5 : Engaged John → (¬ InviteFriends John ∧ ¬ MakePlans John)
-- 6. If John has a larger family, then either John has a well-attended wedding or invites friends to their ceremony.
axiom A6 : LargerFamily John → (WellAttended John ∨ InviteFriends John)

-- From a well-attended wedding for John, we derive a contradiction.
theorem well_attended_contra : WellAttended John → False :=
by
  intro hW
  have hIO : InviteOthers John := A3 John hW
  have hMP : MakePlans John := A2 John hIO
  have hE  : Engaged John := A1 John hMP
  have h := A5 hE
  have hnotMP : ¬ MakePlans John := And.right h
  exact hnotMP hMP

-- From LargerFamily John, we can at least derive InviteFriends John (since the WellAttended branch is impossible).
theorem larger_family_implies_invite_friends : LargerFamily John → InviteFriends John :=
by
  intro hLF
  have hdisj := A6 hLF
  cases hdisj with
  | inl hW =>
      have hf : False := well_attended_contra hW
      exact False.elim hf
  | inr hIF =>
      exact hIF

-- Despite the above, we cannot derive a contradiction from InviteFriends John alone,
-- and there is no rule connecting InviteFriends to MakePlans or Engaged for John.
-- Therefore, we cannot prove ¬ LargerFamily John from the given premises.
-- theorem not_larger_family_John : ¬ LargerFamily John := ?impossible
</lean>

ANSWER: Failure

<lean>
axiom obj : Type
axiom Jane : obj
axiom KiKi : obj

axiom Animal : obj → Prop
axiom Dog : obj → Prop
axiom Bark : obj → Prop
axiom Jump : obj → Prop
axiom Legs : obj → Prop
axiom Terricolous : obj → Prop
axiom Likes : obj → obj → Prop

-- Jane does not like any animals that bark.
axiom H1 : ∀ x : obj, Animal x ∧ Bark x → ¬Likes Jane x
-- All dogs are animals that bark.
axiom H2 : ∀ x : obj, Dog x → Animal x ∧ Bark x
-- Jane likes all animals that jump.
axiom H3 : ∀ x : obj, Animal x ∧ Jump x → Likes Jane x
-- If an animal has legs, then it jumps.
axiom H4 : ∀ x : obj, Animal x ∧ Legs x → Jump x
-- If an animal is terricolous, then it has legs.
axiom H5 : ∀ x : obj, Animal x ∧ Terricolous x → Legs x
-- KiKi is an animal.
axiom H6 : Animal KiKi
-- If KiKi neither jumps nor has legs, then KiKi is terricolous or has legs.
axiom H7 : (¬Jump KiKi ∧ ¬Legs KiKi) → (Terricolous KiKi ∨ Legs KiKi)

open Classical

theorem kiki_no_bark_and_not_dog : ¬Bark KiKi ∧ ¬Dog KiKi := by
  -- First, show KiKi jumps.
  have hJump : Jump KiKi := by
    by_cases hL : Legs KiKi
    · exact H4 KiKi (And.intro H6 hL)
    · by_contra hNotJump
      have hpair : ¬Jump KiKi ∧ ¬Legs KiKi := And.intro hNotJump hL
      have hdisj : Terricolous KiKi ∨ Legs KiKi := H7 hpair
      cases hdisj with
      | inl hTerr =>
        have hlegs : Legs KiKi := H5 KiKi (And.intro H6 hTerr)
        exact hL hlegs
      | inr hlegs =>
        exact hL hlegs
  -- From jumping and being an animal, Jane likes KiKi.
  have hLike : Likes Jane KiKi := H3 KiKi (And.intro H6 hJump)
  -- Therefore KiKi does not bark (else Jane both likes and does not like KiKi).
  have hNotBark : ¬Bark KiKi := by
    intro hBark
    have hNotLike : ¬Likes Jane KiKi := H1 KiKi (And.intro H6 hBark)
    exact hNotLike hLike
  -- From not barking and "dogs bark", KiKi is not a dog.
  have hNotDog : ¬Dog KiKi := by
    intro hDog
    have hbark : Bark KiKi := (And.right (H2 KiKi hDog))
    exact hNotBark hbark
  exact And.intro hNotBark hNotDog
</lean>

ANSWER: True

axiom Algo : Type
axiom Model : Type

axiom SOTA : Model

axiom MLAlgo : Algo → Prop
axiom Supervised : Algo → Prop
axiom Unsupervised : Algo → Prop
axiom Reinforcement : Algo → Prop

axiom UsedToTrain : Model → Algo → Prop
axiom RequiresLabeledData : Algo → Prop

-- Every ML algorithm is either supervised, unsupervised, or reinforcement
axiom CatCov : ∀ a : Algo, MLAlgo a → (Supervised a ∨ Unsupervised a ∨ Reinforcement a)

-- Unsupervised algorithms do not require labeled data
axiom UnsupNoLabels : ∀ a : Algo, Unsupervised a → ¬ RequiresLabeledData a

-- The SOTA model is trained with some ML algorithm
axiom SOTA_trained : ∃ a : Algo, UsedToTrain SOTA a ∧ MLAlgo a

-- Reinforcement learning is not used to train the SOTA model
axiom NoRLforSOTA : ∀ a : Algo, Reinforcement a → ¬ UsedToTrain SOTA a

-- Any algorithm used to train SOTA requires labeled data
axiom TrainRequiresLabel : ∀ a : Algo, UsedToTrain SOTA a → RequiresLabeledData a

-- Conclusion: Some supervised learning algorithm is used to train the SOTA model
theorem supervised_used : ∃ a : Algo, UsedToTrain SOTA a ∧ Supervised a := by
  have ⟨a, h⟩ := SOTA_trained
  have ⟨hUsed, hML⟩ := h
  have hReq : RequiresLabeledData a := TrainRequiresLabel a hUsed
  have hNotUnsup : ¬ Unsupervised a := fun hU => (UnsupNoLabels a hU) hReq
  have hNotReinf : ¬ Reinforcement a := fun hR => (NoRLforSOTA a hR) hUsed
  have hCat : Supervised a ∨ Unsupervised a ∨ Reinforcement a := CatCov a hML
  have hSup : Supervised a := by
    cases hCat with
    | inl hS => exact hS
    | inr hUR =>
      cases hUR with
      | inl hU => exact False.elim (hNotUnsup hU)
      | inr hR => exact False.elim (hNotReinf hR)
  exact ⟨a, And.intro hUsed hSup⟩
</lean>

ANSWER: True

axiom Alg : Type
axiom Unsupervised : Alg → Prop
axiom Supervised : Alg → Prop
axiom Reinforcement : Alg → Prop
axiom RequiresLabeled : Alg → Prop

axiom TrainAlg : Alg

axiom ML_train : ML TrainAlg
-- Unsupervised learning algorithms do not require labeled data
axiom NoLabelsForUnsup : ∀ a : Alg, Unsupervised a → ¬ RequiresLabeled a

axiom RL_not_used : ¬ Reinforcement TrainAlg
-- The ML algorithm for training text summarization models requires labeled data
axiom RequiresLabeled_train : RequiresLabeled TrainAlg
-- ML algorithms can be categorized as supervised, unsupervised, or reinforcement
axiom Categorization : ∀ a : Alg, ML a → (Supervised a ∨ Unsupervised a ∨ Reinforcement a)

-- From the premises we can show the training algorithm is not unsupervised
theorem not_unsupervised_trainalg : ¬ Unsupervised TrainAlg := by
  intro hUnsup
  have hNoLabels : ¬ RequiresLabeled TrainAlg := NoLabelsForUnsup TrainAlg hUnsup
  exact hNoLabels RequiresLabeled_train

  have h := Categorization TrainAlg ML_train
  cases h with
  | inl hSup => exact hSup

    | inl hUnsup =>
      have : False := not_unsupervised_trainalg hUnsup
      exact False.elim this
    | inr hRL =>
      have : False := RL_not_used hRL
      exact False.elim this

-- Cannot prove Unsupervised TrainAlg from given axioms; in fact, its negation holds.
</lean>

axiom ChildrenCanWatchWithoutGuidance : obj → Prop
axiom ContainsExcessiveEroticViolent : obj → Prop
axiom RatedGA : obj → Prop
axiom FamilyFriendlyAnimated : obj → Prop
axiom InFrozenSeries : obj → Prop

-- If a film is appropriate for all ages, then children can watch it without guidance from parents.
axiom R1 : ∀ x : obj, AppropriateAllAges x → ChildrenCanWatchWithoutGuidance x
-- If a film contains excessive erotic and violent content, children cannot watch it without guidance.
axiom R2 : ∀ x : obj, ContainsExcessiveEroticViolent x → ¬ ChildrenCanWatchWithoutGuidance x
-- If a film is rated General Audience, then it is appropriate for all ages.
axiom R3 : ∀ x : obj, RatedGA x → AppropriateAllAges x
-- All family-friendly animated films are rated General Audience.
axiom R4 : ∀ x : obj, FamilyFriendlyAnimated x → RatedGA x
-- All movies in the Frozen series are family-friendly animated films.
axiom R5 : ∀ x : obj, InFrozenSeries x → FamilyFriendlyAnimated x

-- “Hachi: A dog’s Tale” is a film.
axiom Tfilm : Film Hachi
-- “Hachi: A dog’s Tale” is either a family-friendly animated film or is appropriate for all ages.
axiom Tdisj : FamilyFriendlyAnimated Hachi ∨ AppropriateAllAges Hachi

-- From the disjunction, we can derive Hachi is appropriate for all ages.
theorem hachi_appropriate : AppropriateAllAges Hachi := by
  cases Tdisj with
  | inl h =>
      exact R3 Hachi (R4 Hachi h)
  | inr h =>
      exact h

-- Therefore, children can watch Hachi without guidance.
theorem hachi_can_watch : ChildrenCanWatchWithoutGuidance Hachi := by
  exact R1 Hachi hachi_appropriate

-- Hence, Hachi does not contain excessive erotic and violent content.
theorem not_contains_hachi : ¬ ContainsExcessiveEroticViolent Hachi := by
  intro h
  have hnot : ¬ ChildrenCanWatchWithoutGuidance Hachi := R2 Hachi h
  exact hnot hachi_can_watch

-- Target statement: ContainsExcessiveEroticViolent Hachi ∨ InFrozenSeries Hachi
-- We cannot derive this disjunction from the given axioms.
-- Although we proved ¬ContainsExcessiveEroticViolent Hachi, we have no information to conclude InFrozenSeries Hachi.
-- Therefore, the target statement is not provable from the premises.
</lean>

ANSWER: Failure

-- People in this club who perform in school talent shows often attend and are very engaged with school events.
axiom R1 : ∀ x : obj, InClub x ∧ PerformOften x → AttendEngaged x

-- People in this club either perform in school talent shows often or are inactive and disinterested community members.

-- People in this club who chaperone high school dances are not students who attend the school.

-- All people in this club who are inactive and disinterested members of their community chaperone high school dances.
axiom R4 : ∀ x : obj, InClub x ∧ InactiveDisinterested x → Chaperone x

-- All young children and teenagers in this club who wish to further their academic careers and educational opportunities are students who attend the school.

-- and an inactive and disinterested member of the community.
-- Formally:

axiom A3 : ∀ x : Person, InClub x ∧ Chaperone x → ¬ Student x
axiom A4 : ∀ x : Person, InClub x ∧ InactiveDisinterested x → Chaperone x
axiom A5 : ∀ x : Person, InClub x ∧ ((YoungChild x) ∨ (Teenager x)) ∧ WishesAcademic x → Student x
axiom Bonnie_in : InClub Bonnie
axiom Bonnie_dich : (AttendEngaged Bonnie ∧ Student Bonnie) ∨ (¬ AttendEngaged Bonnie ∧ ¬ Student Bonnie)

  ( (((YoungChild Bonnie) ∨ (Teenager Bonnie)) ∧ WishesAcademic Bonnie ∧ Chaperone Bonnie)
    ∨ ¬(((YoungChild Bonnie) ∨ (Teenager Bonnie)) ∧ WishesAcademic Bonnie) ) →
  (Student Bonnie ∨ InactiveDisinterested Bonnie) :=

  cases h with
  | inl hleft =>
      -- hleft : (YorT) ∧ (Wishes ∧ Chaperone)
      have hYorT : (YoungChild Bonnie ∨ Teenager Bonnie) := And.left hleft
      have hWish : WishesAcademic Bonnie := And.left (And.right hleft)
      have hpack : InClub Bonnie ∧ ((YoungChild Bonnie) ∨ (Teenager Bonnie)) ∧ WishesAcademic Bonnie :=
        And.intro Bonnie_in (And.intro hYorT hWish)
      have hstu : Student Bonnie := A5 Bonnie hpack
      exact Or.inl hstu
  | inr _ =>
      -- Use club disjunction
      have hdisj : PerformOften Bonnie ∨ InactiveDisinterested Bonnie := A2 Bonnie Bonnie_in
      cases hdisj with

          exact Or.inr hinact

          have hstu : Student Bonnie :=
            by
              cases Bonnie_dich with
              | inl hp =>

                  have contra : False := hn.left haev
                  exact False.elim contra
          exact Or.inl hstu

axiom obj : Type

-- Predicates
axiom Show : obj → Prop
axiom AirsAt8Mon : obj → Prop
axiom GivesRosesOnTV : obj → Prop
axiom EpisodeOfBachelor : obj → Prop
axiom PortraysRealPeople : obj → Prop
axiom RealityTV : obj → Prop

-- Facts from the context
axiom some_show_airs_gives : ∃ s : obj, Show s ∧ AirsAt8Mon s ∧ GivesRosesOnTV s
axiom gives_implies_bachelor : ∀ x : obj, GivesRosesOnTV x → EpisodeOfBachelor x
axiom bachelor_portrays_real : ∀ x : obj, EpisodeOfBachelor x → PortraysRealPeople x
axiom real_implies_reality : ∀ x : obj, PortraysRealPeople x → RealityTV x

-- Breaking Bad specifics
axiom BreakingBad : obj
axiom BreakingBad_is_show : Show BreakingBad
axiom BreakingBad_not_reality : ¬RealityTV BreakingBad

-- Derived: if a show gives roses on TV, then it's reality TV
theorem roses_implies_reality : ∀ x : obj, GivesRosesOnTV x → RealityTV x :=
by
  intro x h
  have hb : EpisodeOfBachelor x := gives_implies_bachelor x h
  have pr : PortraysRealPeople x := bachelor_portrays_real x hb
  have rt : RealityTV x := real_implies_reality x pr
  exact rt

-- Therefore, Breaking Bad does not give out roses on TV
theorem not_roses_BB : ¬GivesRosesOnTV BreakingBad :=
by
  intro h
  have rt : RealityTV BreakingBad := roses_implies_reality BreakingBad h
  exact BreakingBad_not_reality rt

-- Hence the claimed conjunction cannot hold
theorem claim_unprovable : ¬(Show BreakingBad ∧ AirsAt8Mon BreakingBad ∧ GivesRosesOnTV BreakingBad) :=
by
  intro h
  have g : GivesRosesOnTV BreakingBad := h.right.right
  exact not_roses_BB g
